jobTypes.name: Popcorn/Acoustic Ceiling Removal
jobTypes.summary: Removal of textured acoustic (popcorn) ceiling and refinish to a smooth or knockdown texture, ready for paint.
steps: Test a ceiling sample for asbestos content prior to any disturbance if the home was built before 1980.
Remove furniture from the work area or relocate and cover it in place.
Cover floors, walls, and remaining fixtures with plastic sheeting to contain debris.
Lightly mist the ceiling surface with water to soften the texture and control dust.
Scrape the softened texture from the ceiling down to the substrate.
Refresh plastic containment as needed and re-cover the work area after the bulk of the texture is removed.
Fill and patch cracks, nail pops, and openings in the ceiling substrate.
Apply joint tape and skim-coat plaster over patched areas and seams.
Sand the new plaster smooth.
Apply the customer's chosen finish: smooth or a light knockdown texture.
Sand the finish coat as needed for an even surface.
Apply one coat of PVA or drywall primer to the ceiling.
Apply finish paint to the ceiling.
Remove all plastic containment and clean the work area.
Haul away all debris and texture material.
exclusions: Asbestos abatement (quoted separately if testing is positive)
notes: California AQMD Rule 1403 requires an asbestos survey before disturbing ceiling texture in any home; there is no building-age cutoff, though pre-1980 homes are highest risk.
If asbestos is present, the home must be fully vacated and the area cleared by a certified abatement contractor before Joon's crew re-enters to finish the ceiling.
Job can be sequenced one room at a time so the homeowner can remain in the house, provided no asbestos is present.
Patch-and-paint is a local ceiling finish match only — an exact match to adjoining untouched ceiling texture is not guaranteed.
jobTypes.durationHint: 2-5 days per treated area (excludes any abatement time)

jobTypes.name: Popcorn/Acoustic Ceiling Install
jobTypes.summary: Application or re-application of acoustic/orange-peel ceiling texture, typically to match existing texture after a repair or new drywall install.
steps: Cover floors, walls, and fixtures in the work area with plastic sheeting.
Prepare the ceiling surface for texture: fill and sand cracks, nail pops, and seams.
Mask off adjoining surfaces that are not being textured.
Spray the ceiling with acoustic or orange-peel/eggshell texture material to match the surrounding ceiling.
Allow the texture to cure per manufacturer specifications.
Apply primer to the new texture.
Apply finish paint to match the adjoining ceiling.
Remove plastic containment and clean the work area.
Haul away all debris.
selections: Texture style: smooth, orange peel, eggshell, or knockdown
exclusions: Removal of existing texture (see Popcorn Ceiling Removal)
Structural drywall repair beyond minor patching
notes: Because acoustic texture is applied by hand-spray, an exact seamless match to 20+ year old existing texture cannot be guaranteed — this is a professional match, not a factory match.
If any existing material will be disturbed to prep the surface and it has not been tested for asbestos, testing is required first.

jobTypes.name: Artificial Grass
jobTypes.summary: Removal of existing lawn and installation of a compacted base and artificial turf system, including drainage and border work.
steps: Demolish and remove the existing lawn or ground cover in the install area.
Spray the area with weed killer to eliminate remaining vegetation and roots.
Excavate the ground approximately 3-4 inches across the entire area.
Grade the sub-base and create a slight slope so water drains away from the house.
Compact the exposed ground.
Install a perimeter border to contain the base material and turf edges.
Apply a base layer of crushed rock/gravel mixed with sea sand.
Water the base layer to aid compaction.
Compact the base a second time.
Install a weed/root barrier fabric across the entire base.
Roll out the artificial turf, trimming and seaming pieces as needed to fit the area.
Nail the turf perimeter every 6 inches along all edges.
Nail the turf field as needed to secure the center sections.
Install borders/edging as needed.
Broom in infill sand across the turf surface.
Clean the job site and remove excavated soil.
Haul away all trash and debris.
inclusions: Haul-away of old lawn and dirt
exclusions: Drainage systems beyond surface grading (see French Drain)
notes: Customer selects the turf sample prior to installation; sample approval is required before ordering material.
Grading must direct water away from the foundation — do not create a low point against the house.

jobTypes.name: Air Duct Replacement
jobTypes.summary: Replacement of damaged, disconnected, or asbestos-wrapped attic/crawlspace air ducts with new insulated flexible ductwork, reconnected to the existing HVAC system.
steps: Cover all working areas to protect the attic access path and interior finishes.
Inspect existing ductwork and identify sections requiring repair vs. full replacement.
For minor repairs: seal duct seams with foil duct tape and apply mastic sealant over the taped joints.
For full replacement: disconnect and remove all old ductwork from the attic or crawlspace.
Haul away old ducts and any deteriorated insulation wrap.
If old ducts contain asbestos wrap, coordinate removal with a certified asbestos abatement sub prior to Joon's crew re-entering.
Install new insulated flexible R-6 air duct runs sized to the existing system.
Install elbows and transitions at direction changes as needed.
Connect new ducts to the existing plenum/trunk and to each supply/return register.
Seal all duct connections with mastic and clean all filters and register boots.
Perform an airflow test at each register.
Final inspection.
exclusions: Asbestos abatement of old duct wrap (quoted separately if present)
notes: If existing ducts or wrap material are suspected asbestos-containing, a certified abatement contractor must remove them before Joon's HVAC crew proceeds — a free air-clearance test will confirm the area is safe to re-enter.

jobTypes.name: HVAC Changeout / Heat Pump Conversion
jobTypes.summary: Replacement of an end-of-life furnace/AC system with new equipment sized to the house, including a new outdoor condenser pad, refrigerant lines, and electrical connection — or full conversion to an electric heat pump system.
steps: Cover the work area and protect flooring along the equipment path.
Size the replacement unit to the square footage and layout of the house (2.5 through 5-ton range).
Remove and dispose of the existing furnace/coil/condenser.
Install the new furnace and/or evaporator coil in the equipment closet or attic.
Run new copper refrigerant lines between the indoor coil and outdoor condenser.
Pour a new concrete equipment pad for the outdoor condenser.
Set the new condenser unit on the pad.
Run a dedicated electrical circuit and disconnect box to the condenser location.
Connect refrigerant lines between coil and condenser and pressure-test for leaks.
Connect and test thermostat wiring and controls.
For heat pump conversions: verify electrical panel capacity and add a circuit/breaker as needed (see Electrical Panel Upgrade if capacity is insufficient).
Charge the system with refrigerant per manufacturer specification.
Test heating and cooling cycles for proper operation.
Clean the work area and haul away the old equipment.
Final inspection.
selections: Gas furnace vs. electric heat pump
inclusions: New furnace/AC or heat pump equipment
exclusions: Electrical panel upgrade if existing panel lacks capacity (quoted separately)
notes: Heat pump conversions increase electrical load — confirm panel capacity before quoting; a 100A panel is often insufficient once a heat pump, EV charger, and induction range are combined.

jobTypes.name: Bathroom Remodel
jobTypes.summary: Full gut-and-rebuild of a bathroom — new plumbing, electrical, waterproofing, tile, and fixtures — to a layout and finish package selected by the customer.
steps: Pull city building, plumbing, and electrical permits.
Protect adjoining areas and prepare the work area.
Demolish the existing shower/tub, tile, and flooring down to the studs and subfloor as needed; clean up and haul away debris.
Upgrade all supply plumbing within the bathroom to copper, including new shut-off valves at the shower, toilet, and vanity.
Replace the drain P-trap and shower/tub drain to current code.
Repair or replace framing and subfloor where damaged, and frame any customer-requested niche or bench.
Set a new shower pan (or install a new tub) and connect drain and overflow.
For shower pans: hot-mop the pan and perform a 24-hour standing water test before covering with tile.
Rough in electrical: ceiling exhaust fan, GFCI-protected outlets, and light fixtures (including shower-rated fixtures where applicable).
Install waterproof membrane paper and wire lath (chicken wire) on all walls and floor areas to be tiled.
Apply a scratch coat of cement over the paper and wire lath in preparation for tile.
Install the customer-selected tile on walls and floor, including niche/bench if applicable.
Apply grout and a penetrating sealer to the tile.
Repair drywall throughout the bathroom, then prime and paint.
Install the vanity with countertop and sink, and connect the faucet.
Install the toilet with a new wax ring.
Install the medicine cabinet and mirror per customer selection.
Install grab bars if requested.
Install the shower head, valve trim, and all remaining fixtures.
Install the shower door or curtain rod.
Install cabinet/vanity hardware and new baseboards.
Final cleaning of the job site.
Final inspection.
selections: Shower type: tub, standard shower, walk-in shower, roll-in shower, walk-in tub, or fiberglass unit
inclusions: Rough material (copper pipe, wire lath, cement, hot mop)
exclusions: Vanity, countertop, and fixtures (customer selection; budget separately)
notes: Homes built before 1978 require a lead survey, and homes with original materials should be tested for asbestos before demolition begins.
Shower pans must pass a 24-hour standing water test before tile installation — no exceptions.
Set clear vanity, countertop, tile, and fixture budgets with the customer before framing begins to avoid mid-job selection delays.
Customer selections for tile, vanity, and fixtures must be finalized before the rough plumbing/electrical stage to avoid schedule delays.

jobTypes.name: Walk-In Tub / Fiberglass Shower Unit
jobTypes.summary: Removal of an existing tub/shower and installation of a customer-selected walk-in tub or one-piece fiberglass shower unit.
steps: Demolish and remove the existing tub or shower unit.
Prepare and rough in plumbing for the new unit, including a new drain and copper supply lines.
For walk-in tubs: run a dedicated GFCI-protected circuit for the tub's pump/heater equipment.
Widen or adjust the door frame opening if the new unit requires a larger entry, then reinstall trim.
Set the new walk-in tub or fiberglass shower unit in place and level it.
Connect drain, overflow, and supply lines to the unit.
Install tile or a surround panel around the unit as applicable.
Seal all joints between the unit and surrounding walls/floor.
Test fill and drain cycles, and for walk-in tubs, test jets/heater function.
Clean the job site and haul away debris.
selections: Walk-in tub model (door side, jets, heated seat options) or fiberglass shower design, chosen from catalog
exclusions: The tub/shower unit itself (customer selection; budget separately unless quoted with fixture included)
notes: Walk-in tubs are large — the door frame frequently must be widened and later restored to fit the unit through the doorway; confirm door width before quoting.
Because a walk-in tub fills and drains slowly, discuss expected fill/drain times with the customer before installation.

jobTypes.name: Blueprints & Plans
jobTypes.summary: Preparation of measured drawings, engineering, and city-ready construction documents for a room addition, ADU, or other permitted structural project.
steps: Measure the existing house and photograph all relevant areas.
Measure and design the proposed addition or scope of work.
Prepare a property line survey and mark boundaries relevant to the project.
Engineer and calculate framing, support, and load requirements for the new construction.
Prepare a preliminary sketch/layout for the customer to review and approve.
Revise the sketch based on customer feedback and obtain sign-off.
Prepare full construction blueprints for submittal to the city.
Prepare a soil report and geological report if required by the jurisdiction.
Submit plans to the city and respond to plan-check corrections as needed.
Provide the customer with the required number of approved plan copies.
notes: Plan-check processing time varies by jurisdiction and can take 1-4 months to complete — build this into the customer's overall project timeline expectations.
jobTypes.durationHint: 4-16 weeks depending on city plan-check turnaround

jobTypes.name: Foundation Seismic Retrofit (Bolting)
jobTypes.summary: Seismic strengthening of the foundation and cripple wall — anchor bolting, framing connectors, and plywood shear bracing — to reduce earthquake damage risk.
steps: Pull the city building permit for the seismic retrofit.
Access the crawlspace/foundation perimeter (remove exterior stucco locally if crawlspace clearance is under 18 inches).
Install foundation anchor bolts connecting the sill plate to the foundation at a maximum of 6 feet on center.
Install Simpson (or equivalent) foundation plates with bolts into the foundation and lag screws into the sill plate every 4 feet around the house perimeter.
Replace or adjust deteriorated concrete piers and 4x4 posts as needed.
Install metal straps connecting existing and new posts to the piers and framing above.
Rebuild or reinforce the cripple wall with new blocking top and bottom and studs every 16 inches on center.
Install structural plywood shear panels on the cripple wall per code, covering corners first and additional panels up to the required percentage of wall length.
Install metal straps connecting the plywood to the wood blocking every 24 inches.
Install framing anchors at all post-to-beam and beam-to-pier connections per code.
Patch any exterior stucco that was opened for crawlspace access.
Clean the work area and haul away debris.
Final inspection.
exclusions: Jacking or leveling the house (see Foundation Leveling)
Rotted wood/sill plate replacement beyond minor spot repair (quoted separately)
notes: If crawlspace clearance is under 18 inches, exterior stucco must be removed to bolt from outside — include stucco patch-back in the scope and price.
This retrofit often qualifies homeowners for the CEA Earthquake Brace + Bolt (EBB) grant and insurance premium credits — advise the customer to check eligibility before work begins.
Work does not include re-leveling a house that has already settled; that is a separate foundation crack/underpinning scope.

jobTypes.name: Foundation Leveling & Crack Repair
jobTypes.summary: Correction of a settled or unlevel foundation by jacking the structure back into position and reinforcing the pier-and-post support system.
steps: Inspect the crawlspace and foundation condition throughout the affected area.
Raise the house with hydraulic jacks in small, controlled increments, up to approximately 12 inches as needed.
Reinforce piers with new 2x4 cribbing and corner brackets during the lift.
Reinforce floor joist-to-pier and post-to-pier connections.
Replace or add concrete piers/footings where deteriorated or undersized.
Lower the house back into its corrected position and remove the jacks.
Re-level windows and doors affected by the settlement.
Patch cracks in interior and exterior finishes caused by the settlement, as needed.
Sand patched areas smooth.
Prime and paint patched areas to match existing finish as closely as possible.
Clean the work area and haul away debris.
Final inspection.
exclusions: Seismic bolting/shear plywood (see Foundation Seismic Retrofit)
Full interior repaint beyond patched areas
notes: Patch-and-paint after leveling is a local finish match only — an exact match to existing paint/texture cannot be guaranteed, especially on aged or sun-faded finishes.
Active, ongoing settlement (vs. historical, stable settlement) may require a geotechnical evaluation before a leveling scope can be finalized.

jobTypes.name: Block Wall
jobTypes.summary: Construction of a new concrete masonry unit (CMU) block wall for a property-line enclosure, privacy screen, or low garden wall.
steps: Demolish and haul away any existing wall or fence in the footprint.
Excavate a footing trench sized to the wall height and local code.
Build a wood form template for the footing.
Install reinforcing rebar in the footing per code.
Pour concrete for the footing.
Install the first course of CMU block and continue coursing to the design height, filling and reinforcing cells per code.
Apply grout/cement fill in reinforced cells.
Finish the wall face per the customer's selected color and block style (split-face, stucco-coat, or capped).
Install cap blocks along the top of the wall.
Clean the entire job site and haul away debris.
exclusions: Stucco color-coat finish beyond a standard block face (if requested as an upgrade)
notes: Walls over the height permitted by local code without a permit (typically 6 feet, less near street-facing setbacks) require engineering and a building permit — verify local zoning before quoting.
Property line location should be confirmed by survey before excavation begins to avoid encroachment disputes.

jobTypes.name: Countertop Replacement (Slab & Tile)
jobTypes.summary: Removal of existing counters and installation of new kitchen or bathroom countertops in slab (granite/quartz/Caesarstone) or tile, without a full remodel.
steps: Take detailed measurements of the existing countertop for fabrication.
Cover cabinets with heavy-duty plastic and protect flooring from the cabinet area to the front door.
Demolish the existing countertop and backsplash and disconnect the sink plumbing; clean up and haul away debris.
Install new 3/4-inch plywood substrate over the cabinets.
For slab countertops: fabricate and cut the customer-selected slab, including the sink cutout and edge profile (bull-nose or other selected edge).
For tile countertops: build up the substrate with wire lath, pour a 2,500 PSI concrete mud bed, and let it cure before setting tile.
Install the new countertop surface — slab set with epoxy adhesive, or tile set with spacers per the selected pattern.
For tile: apply grout in the customer's selected color.
Apply a penetrating sealer to the countertop (grout only for tile; full surface for porous stone slabs).
Install the backsplash in the selected height (4-inch, 6-inch, or full-height) and material.
Install the sink, garbage disposal, and faucet, and reconnect all plumbing.
Install GFCI outlets along the backsplash per code (kitchen only).
Reinstall appliances that were disconnected for the work.
Clean the entire job site.
selections: Slab material and edge profile, or tile pattern and grout color
exclusions: The countertop slab/tile material itself unless quoted with material included
Sink and faucet (customer selection unless included)
notes: Customer must finalize slab or tile selection before fabrication/ordering — lead times for custom slabs commonly run 1-3 weeks.

jobTypes.name: Carpet Installation
jobTypes.summary: Removal of existing flooring and installation of new wall-to-wall carpet and padding.
steps: Move furniture out of the work area or relocate within the room.
Cover adjoining hard-surface areas to protect them during installation.
Remove the existing carpet and padding; replace wood tack strips as necessary.
Scrape and clean the subfloor surface.
Allow the subfloor to dry fully if any moisture is present.
Install new tack strips along the room perimeter.
Install new carpet padding.
Install the customer-selected carpet, seaming as needed for the room size.
Stretch and secure the carpet with a power stretcher, stapling and gluing edges as needed.
Trim and finish carpet edges at doorways and transitions.
Clean the entire job site.
Return furniture to its original position.
selections: Carpet style, color, and pad thickness (from samples)
notes: Carpet must be selected from samples before ordering; lead time varies by style and availability.

jobTypes.name: Concrete Driveway / Walkway / Steps
jobTypes.summary: Demolition of an existing driveway/walkway and installation of a new reinforced concrete surface, with optional decorative finish.
steps: Demolish the existing concrete or asphalt surface and haul away debris.
Excavate the surface 4-6 inches for the new concrete section.
Grade and compact the sub-base, creating a slope for water to drain away from the house.
Install wood forms and dividers to define the new concrete area and control joints.
Install rebar or wire mesh reinforcement, lifted approximately 2 inches off the ground.
Pour 2,500-3,000 PSI concrete at a 4-inch minimum thickness.
Finish the surface per the customer's selection: smooth, broom, salt, or stamped finish.
Apply color/stain if selected.
Apply a concrete sealer (optional upgrade).
Remove the wood forms once the concrete has cured sufficiently.
Clean the entire job site and haul away all debris.
selections: Finish style: smooth, broom, salt, or stamped
exclusions: Brick or decorative border framing (extra cost)
notes: Grading must slope water away from the foundation, not toward it.
Driveway approach work at the public right-of-way may require a separate city encroachment permit.

jobTypes.name: Driveway / Walkway Pavers
jobTypes.summary: Installation of an interlocking paver driveway or walkway over a compacted gravel base, as a new install or replacement of an existing slab/asphalt surface.
steps: Demolish the existing driveway/walkway surface and haul away debris.
Excavate the sub-base 4-6 inches.
Grade and compact the sub-base, creating a slope for water to drain away from the house.
Apply and compact a gravel/sand base layer.
Install a concrete or paver-restraint edge around the perimeter.
Install pavers one by one in the customer-selected pattern.
Apply joint sand and sweep it into the paver joints.
Compact the paver surface with a plate compactor to lock the joint sand.
Clean the paver surface.
Apply a paver sealer (optional upgrade).
Clean the entire job site and haul away debris.
selections: Paver style, color, and laying pattern
inclusions: Base gravel/sand and pavers (or per allowance)
notes: Pavers are a permeable-friendly surface option that some SoCal jurisdictions favor for stormwater compliance — flag this to customers doing hardscape near setbacks.

jobTypes.name: Asphalt Driveway
jobTypes.summary: Demolition of an existing driveway surface and installation of a new compacted asphalt driveway.
steps: Demolish the existing asphalt or concrete surface and haul away debris.
Grade and compact the sub-base.
Apply a 1/2-inch gravel base layer and compact.
Apply 3 inches of hot-mix asphalt.
Compact the asphalt with a roller.
Clean the entire job site and haul away all debris.
exclusions: Sealcoating (extra cost, recommended after initial cure)
notes: Fresh asphalt should not bear vehicle weight for the manufacturer-specified cure period — advise the customer accordingly.

jobTypes.name: Fence: Vinyl
jobTypes.summary: Removal of an existing fence and installation of a new vinyl privacy or picket fence on concrete-set posts.
steps: Demolish the existing fence and haul away debris.
Rough in wiring for any customer-requested post lighting.
Excavate 18-inch by 18-inch concrete pier holes for each post location.
Set vinyl posts and fill with concrete (or a 4x4 wood core inside the vinyl sleeve).
Install horizontal vinyl rails between posts.
Install vinyl privacy or picket panels between rails.
Install post caps and any selected post lighting.
Clean the entire job site and haul away debris.
inclusions: Vinyl fence material, posts, and concrete
exclusions: Gate hardware upgrades beyond standard latch/hinges
Electrical service for lighting (only the fence wiring run is included)
notes: Fences over 6 feet (or lower near street-facing front yards, per local zoning) typically require a building permit — verify height limits with the city before quoting.
Confirm property line location before excavating post holes.

jobTypes.name: Fence: Wood
jobTypes.summary: Removal of an existing fence and installation of a new wood privacy fence on concrete-set posts.
steps: Demolish the existing fence and haul away debris.
Excavate 18-inch by 18-inch concrete pier holes for each post location.
Set 4x4 posts on 8-foot centers and pour concrete footings.
Install a 2x4 rail frame between posts.
Install 1x6 wood fence boards (Douglas fir or redwood) on the facing side.
Stain and varnish, or prime and paint, the completed fence.
Clean the entire job site and haul away debris.
notes: Fences over 6 feet (or lower near street-facing front yards, per local zoning) typically require a building permit — verify height limits with the city before quoting.

jobTypes.name: Fence: Wrought Iron
jobTypes.summary: Removal of an existing fence and installation of a new wrought iron fence on concrete-set posts.
steps: Demolish the existing fence and haul away debris.
Excavate 18-inch by 18-inch concrete pier holes for each post location.
Set iron posts and pour concrete footings.
Install horizontal iron rails between posts.
Install the wrought iron fence panels in the customer-selected design.
Prime and finish-coat any field-welded or cut sections.
Clean the entire job site and haul away debris.
notes: Fences over 6 feet (or lower near street-facing front yards, per local zoning) typically require a building permit — verify height limits with the city before quoting.

jobTypes.name: Flooring: Hardwood / Engineered Wood
jobTypes.summary: Removal of existing flooring and installation of new solid hardwood, engineered wood, or bamboo flooring, glued or nailed per material and subfloor type.
steps: Move furniture out of the work area.
Cover adjoining areas to protect them during installation.
Remove the existing flooring and haul away debris.
Test the subfloor surface for moisture.
On a concrete slab: apply a heavy-duty moisture sealer. On a raised foundation: install 15-year-rated tar paper over the subfloor.
Install a cork underlayment pad if required by the selected product.
On a raised foundation, install plywood underlayment before the finish floor.
Apply flooring adhesive to the substrate — two layers of glue for solid hardwood on slab, one layer for engineered wood.
Install the customer-selected wood, engineered wood, or bamboo flooring piece by piece, glued or nailed per the manufacturer's specification for the substrate type.
Install new baseboards and quarter-round/half-round trim around the perimeter.
Prime and paint the new baseboards.
Clean the wood floor surface with the manufacturer-recommended cleaner.
Return furniture to the room.
Clean the entire work area.
selections: Wood species/engineered product and finish (from samples)
notes: Solid hardwood over a concrete slab requires two layers of adhesive and is more moisture-sensitive than engineered wood — flag this to customers on slab foundations considering solid hardwood.
Subfloor moisture must test within acceptable range before installation; elevated readings require remediation first.

jobTypes.name: Flooring: Laminate
jobTypes.summary: Removal of existing flooring and installation of new laminate or waterproof laminate/vinyl plank flooring.
steps: Move furniture out of the work area.
Remove the existing flooring and haul away debris.
Test the subfloor for moisture.
Install a foam or cork underlayment pad across the subfloor.
Install the customer-selected laminate or waterproof laminate flooring using a floating-floor method.
Install new baseboards and quarter-round/half-round trim around the perimeter.
Clean the entire work area and haul away debris.
Return furniture to the room.
notes: Waterproof laminate/LVP is the recommended upgrade for kitchens, bathrooms, and entries where moisture exposure is likely.

jobTypes.name: Flooring: Tile
jobTypes.summary: Removal of existing flooring and installation of new ceramic, porcelain, or natural stone tile flooring set over a floating mud bed or cement backer board.
steps: Cover the work area as needed.
Move furniture out of the work area.
Remove the existing flooring and haul away debris.
Repair or level the subfloor as needed.
Install cement backer board or a floating mud bed as the tile substrate.
Install the customer-selected tile with spacers, following the chosen pattern.
Apply grout in the seams in the customer's selected color.
Apply a penetrating sealer to the grout.
Install baseboards to match the new floor height.
Clean the entire work area and haul away debris.
Return furniture to the room.
selections: Tile size, style, and pattern (customer to supply tile or select from allowance)
inclusions: Rough material: backer board, thinset, and grout
exclusions: Tile material itself (customer-supplied, or per allowance)
notes: Tile is typically customer-supplied or purchased under an allowance built into the contract — set the allowance amount clearly before work begins.

jobTypes.name: Flooring: Linoleum
jobTypes.summary: Removal of existing flooring and installation of new glue-down linoleum/sheet vinyl flooring.
steps: Remove the existing flooring and haul away debris.
Prepare the substrate with a cement patch/skim coat as needed for a smooth surface.
Install the customer-selected linoleum sheet, glued down to the substrate.
Roll the flooring to remove air bubbles and ensure full adhesion.
Install quarter-round/half-round trim against the existing baseboard.
Clean the entire work area and haul away debris.

jobTypes.name: Insulation (Attic, Wall & Under-House)
jobTypes.summary: Removal of old/contaminated insulation and installation of new insulation in the attic, exterior walls, and/or under-house crawlspace to improve energy efficiency and comfort.
steps: Cover all working areas to protect finishes.
Remove existing insulation from the attic, walls, or crawlspace and haul away debris.
Disinfect the treated area with an antimicrobial fogging treatment to address bacteria and mold before installing new material.
Seal attic penetrations and gaps around wiring, plumbing, and ductwork.
Attic: install new R-38 batt or blown insulation across the attic floor, working around ducts and cables.
Walls (exterior access, no drywall removal): drill access holes from the exterior and blow in R-13 insulation every 16 inches on center, then patch and paint the exterior.
Walls (interior access): remove drywall, install R-13 batt insulation between studs, then patch, prime, and paint the drywall.
Under-house: dry out any existing moisture in the crawlspace before installing insulation.
Under-house: install batt insulation between floor joists and staple it to the framing.
Replace foundation vent screens disturbed during the work.
Clean the entire work area and haul away debris.
selections: Areas to be treated: attic, walls, and/or under-house
Insulation R-value upgrade (optional beyond code minimum)
exclusions: Drywall replacement beyond patch repair for wall-insulation access
notes: Wall insulation via exterior drill-and-fill avoids opening interior drywall but leaves small patched holes in the exterior stucco/siding — a local match only, not a seamless repaint.
If existing insulation shows signs of rodent activity or heavy contamination, full removal and disinfection is recommended before new material goes in.

jobTypes.name: Kitchen Full Remodel
jobTypes.summary: Complete kitchen overhaul — new layout, cabinets, countertops, plumbing, electrical, and finishes — from demolition through final walkthrough.
steps: Prepare a 3D design sketch for customer approval covering floor, cabinet finish, backsplash, and countertop selections.
Pull city building, plumbing, and electrical permits.
Cover all working areas.
Demolish and remove existing cabinets, countertops, backsplash, floor tile, and baseboards; clean up and haul away debris.
Verify whether any wall being altered is load-bearing or contains plumbing/electrical before demolition proceeds.

Replace all outlets and rough in new electrical: recessed lights, under-cabinet lighting, ceiling lights, GFCI outlets every 3 feet along the counter, switches, dimmers, and dedicated circuits for the dishwasher and garbage disposal.
Replace supply plumbing in walls with copper piping up to code, and update drain vents to current code.
Prepare range hood ducting (pipe and panels) if a vented hood is planned.
Repair or replace drywall (fire-rated board at the range wall where required) and patch, prime, and paint.
Install the customer-selected cabinets.
Install the countertop, sink, faucet, dishwasher air gap/diverter, and garbage disposal.
Install the backsplash, then apply grout and sealer.
Install tar paper and cement board on raised-foundation subfloors, then install the new floor material.
Apply grout and sealer to tile flooring if selected.
Connect and test all plumbing fixtures.
Reinstall or install new appliances (customer-provided) and connect utilities.
Install the range hood or microwave-hood combination.
Install cabinet door and drawer hardware.
Complete final touch-ups and clean the job site.
Final inspection.
exclusions: Cabinet, countertop, tile, and fixture material (customer selection; budget separately)
notes: If demolishing a soffit or any wall, verify there is no load-bearing framing or hidden plumbing before cutting.

Customer selections for cabinets, countertop, and tile must be finalized before cabinet fabrication begins — custom cabinets take up to 3 weeks to build and up to 3 weeks to replace if damaged in transit; semi-custom cabinets take about 5 days.
Pre-1978 homes require a lead survey before demolition.

jobTypes.name: Kitchen Refacing & Refinishing
jobTypes.summary: A kitchen refresh that keeps the existing cabinet boxes — either refacing with new doors/veneer or refinishing the existing doors with new paint or stain.
steps: Cover all working areas.
Remove all cabinet doors and drawer fronts.
Sand the cabinet box faces and existing doors (refinish scope).
Patch cabinet face imperfections with caulk and re-sand.
Refacing: cover cabinet box faces with new veneer, Formica, or thermofoil, and build new solid-wood or melamine doors and drawer fronts to the customer's selected style.
Refinishing: apply the customer's selected finish — primer and paint (lacquer or standard) or stain and varnish/clear coat — to the existing doors and cabinet boxes.
Install new (refacing) or existing refinished (refinishing) doors and drawer fronts.
Build and install new drawer boxes if included in the refacing scope.
Install new hardware, hinges, and knobs/pulls per customer selection.
Clean the entire work area and haul away debris.
selections: Refacing material: veneer, Formica, or thermofoil, and new door style
exclusions: Countertop or backsplash replacement (see Countertop Replacement)
Full cabinet box replacement (see Kitchen Remodel)
notes: Refacing/refinishing works only when the existing cabinet boxes are structurally sound — flag water damage or warping to the customer before quoting, since it may require full replacement instead.

jobTypes.name: Mold Removal & Remediation
jobTypes.summary: Testing, containment, removal, and clearance testing for visible mold growth traced to a leak, poor ventilation, or chronic moisture.
steps: Perform an initial mold test and obtain a lab report identifying the mold type and extent.
Cover and seal the affected area to fully contain it from the rest of the house.
Demolish and remove contaminated materials (drywall, plywood, insulation, etc.) from the affected area.
Install a negative-air machine exhausting to the exterior, run continuously for a minimum of 3 days to starve remaining spores of oxygen.
Sand all contaminated framing and remaining surfaces as needed.
Disinfect the entire contained area with an antimicrobial treatment.
Clean up and haul away all contaminated debris.
Order a clearance air test and obtain a lab report confirming the area is safe.
Repair the affected area: replace drywall, insulation, and finishes as needed.
Patch, prime, and paint repaired surfaces.
selections: None for the remediation scope; finish selections apply to the repair phase
exclusions: Repair of the root moisture source (e.g., roof leak, plumbing leak — quoted as a separate scope)
Permit and city fees where applicable
notes: The entire job must be performed by crew in full protective suits and respirators.
Common mold locations in SoCal homes: bathroom walls, kitchen sink walls, water heater closets, laundry rooms, and A/C/furnace closets — inspect these areas even when the visible mold is elsewhere.
Repair of the area (drywall, paint) must be quoted and charged separately from the remediation itself — don't bundle it into the abatement price.

jobTypes.name: Interior Paint
jobTypes.summary: Preparation and repainting of interior walls, ceilings, and/or trim — refresh, color change, or pre-listing touch-up.
steps: Move furniture as needed and cover remaining furniture and flooring.
Prepare all working areas, including masking of trim, outlets, and fixtures not being painted.
Open hairline cracks slightly wider and fill them, along with any holes or dents.
Fix and patch all wall damage.
Sand patched areas and existing walls as needed.
Apply one coat of primer to patched areas and any color-change walls.
Apply two coats of finish paint to walls (and ceiling, if included).
Paint trim and baseboards if included in the scope.
Clean up and haul away debris.
Return furniture and the room to its original layout.
selections: Paint brand (Benjamin Moore, Sherwin-Williams, Dunn-Edwards, Behr, or other) and color, by room
notes: Patch-and-paint on an existing painted wall is a local match only if just a section is repainted; painting the full wall/room ensures a uniform color.
If the home was built before 1978, any sanding or scraping of existing painted surfaces triggers EPA RRP lead-safe work practice requirements.

jobTypes.name: Exterior Paint
jobTypes.summary: Power washing, surface prep, stucco crack repair, and repainting of the exterior of the house.
steps: Cover all working areas — windows, trees, planters, bushes, cars, and adjoining surfaces.
Power wash the entire house at approximately 2,500 PSI.
Excavate a 4-6 inch trench around the exterior walls where accessible to expose the base of the stucco.
Scrape and remove damaged/loose stucco.
Sand all exterior wood trim.
Replace damaged wood trim as identified and agreed with the customer (specify quantity and locations in the contract).
Open hairline stucco cracks slightly wider to accept patch material.
Fill cracks and apply stucco patch repair to damaged areas.
Apply caulking and bondo filler to trim as needed.
Seal all openings and re-caulk around windows and doors.
Apply one coat of primer to stucco and trim.
Apply two coats of finish paint to the stucco (color selected by customer).
Apply two coats of finish paint to trim (oil-based, color selected by customer).
Backfill the trench around the house.
Remove all masking and plastic.
Clean the entire job site.
selections: Paint brand (Benjamin Moore, Sherwin-Williams, Dunn-Edwards, Behr, or other) and color
exclusions: Full re-stucco of large failed areas (see Re-Stucco)
Wood trim replacement beyond the quantity specified in the contract
notes: Homes built before 1978 require lead-safe work practices whenever existing exterior paint is scraped or sanded.
Patch-and-paint stucco repairs are a local color and texture match only; an exact match to sun-faded existing stucco cannot be guaranteed.

jobTypes.name: Trim Paint
jobTypes.summary: Preparation and repainting of exterior trim — fascia boards, eaves, and window/door casings — without a full house repaint.
steps: Power wash the trim areas around the house.
Cover the work area.
Mask all windows and doors adjoining the trim.
Sand all exterior wood trim: fascia board, eaves, and molding around windows and door frames.
Patch damaged wood as needed.
Replace severely damaged wood trim sections as needed.
Apply one coat of primer to all trim.
Apply two coats of finish paint.
Clean the entire work area.
exclusions: Wood trim replacement beyond minor patching (quoted separately)
notes: Homes built before 1978 require lead-safe work practices whenever existing exterior paint is scraped or sanded.

jobTypes.name: Texcote / Cool Wall Coating
jobTypes.summary: Application of a textured, elastomeric cool-coat system over stucco to improve crack resistance and reduce exterior surface temperature.
steps: Cover all windows, doors, trees, bushes, and planters.
Excavate a 6-inch trench around the house where accessible.
Wash the house with a hydro-blast high-pressure machine at approximately 2,500 PSI.
Prepare the stucco surface: fix cracks and patch damaged areas as needed.
Apply a primer coat over the stucco.
Apply one coat of the elastomeric cool-coat material.
Prepare trim for painting: sand, patch, and caulk as needed.
Apply primer to all trim.
Apply two coats of finish paint to trim.
Backfill the trench and clean the entire job site.
exclusions: Full stucco replacement for extensively failed walls (see Re-Stucco)
notes: Cool-wall coatings are elastomeric and bridge hairline cracks better than standard paint, but they are not a substitute for repairing structurally failed stucco.

jobTypes.name: Patio Cover: Wood
jobTypes.summary: Construction of a new wood patio cover — solid waterproof roof or open lattice style — attached to the house on a new concrete-footed post foundation.
steps: Demolish the existing patio cover, if any, and haul away debris.
Prepare and compact the surface for new concrete post footings.
Install rebar or wire mesh reinforcement approximately 2 inches off the ground in each footing.
Prepare brackets/braces for each patio post.
Pour 2,500 PSI concrete footings.
Finish the concrete surface (smooth, salt, or broom finish).
Install 4x4 or 4x6 posts per the customer's selected layout.
Install a ledger plate anchored to the house stucco/framing.
For lattice covers: install lattice framing between the house plate and posts.
Install angled bracing between the patio cover structure and posts.
Install 3/4-inch plywood decking on top of the framing (solid roof style).
Install a waterproof roofing layer — torch-down hot mop or composition shingles over 30-lb tar paper — on solid roof covers.
Stain and varnish, or prime and paint, the finished patio cover.
Install a ceiling fan/light fixture and switch if electrical was selected.
Hose down the patio cover to test waterproofing.
Relocate any electrical panel/box that falls within the new structure's footprint.
Clean up and haul away debris.
Final inspection.
selections: Wood species (Douglas fir, redwood, or finished lumber)
exclusions: Electrical service upgrade beyond the fan/light circuit
notes: Any electrical panel or meter box located within the new cover's footprint must be relocated before construction — this is a code requirement, not optional.
Patio covers attached to the house require a building permit in nearly every SoCal jurisdiction.

jobTypes.name: Patio Cover: Aluminum
jobTypes.summary: Construction of a new aluminum patio cover — flat pan, W-shape, or insulated R-40 panel — attached to the house on a new concrete-footed post foundation.
steps: Demolish the existing patio cover, if any, and haul away debris.
Prepare and compact the surface for new concrete post footings.
Install rebar or wire mesh reinforcement approximately 2 inches off the ground in each footing.
Prepare brackets/braces for each patio post.
Pour 2,500 PSI concrete footings.
Finish the concrete surface (smooth, salt, or broom finish).
Install aluminum posts sized for the selected cover style (flat pan or W-shape).
Install an aluminum mounting track on the house stucco.
Install the patio cover panels in the customer's selected style: flat pan, W-shape, or 3-inch insulated R-40 (insulated panels include a built-in rain gutter).
Install skylight panels if selected.
Install rain gutter downspouts.
Apply 50-year rated caulking on all joints for upper-level waterproofing.
Install a ceiling fan/light fixture and switch if electrical was selected.
Relocate any electrical panel/box that falls within the new structure's footprint.
Clean up and haul away debris.
Final inspection.
selections: Panel style: flat pan, W-shape, or insulated R-40
inclusions: Aluminum posts, track, and cover panels
exclusions: Electrical service upgrade beyond the fan/light circuit
notes: Any electrical panel or meter box located within the new cover's footprint must be relocated before construction.
Insulated R-40 panels include a built-in rain gutter — standard flat pan/W-shape covers need separate gutters installed.

jobTypes.name: Patio Enclosure / Sunroom
jobTypes.summary: Conversion of an existing open patio cover into an enclosed sunroom by infilling the perimeter with window walls and doors.
steps: Confirm the enclosure design meets the 60% minimum window/opening requirement, with wall sections framed at 2, 3, or 4 inches thick.
Confirm window glazing is single or double pane, insulated, with optional Low-E glass, in an aluminum frame.
Install mounting tracks on the concrete slab and existing patio cover structure for the new enclosure walls.
Install the patio enclosure wall sections in the customer-selected finish and color.
Rough in electrical supply as needed for outlets, air conditioning, and lighting.
Install French doors or the customer's selected door style.
Clean up and haul away debris.
Final inspection.
exclusions: HVAC equipment (ducting/mini-split unit quoted separately if added)
notes: The 60% window/opening requirement is a common jurisdictional threshold that keeps the structure classified as a patio enclosure rather than a habitable room addition — verify the exact requirement with the local building department before finalizing the design.

jobTypes.name: Pool Re-Plaster & Equipment
jobTypes.summary: Replastering of a worn or cracked pool/spa shell and/or replacement of aging pump, filter, and heater equipment.
steps: Empty the pool and spa of all water.
Demolish/chip 2-3 inches of the existing plaster using a high-pressure compressor, clean up, and haul away debris.
Demolish the existing waterline tile, clean up, and haul away debris.
Install new tile at the waterline (customer to provide tile).
Pour and hand-trowel new plaster (color selected by customer), finishing smooth from top to bottom before it sets — the entire shell must be plastered in a single continuous pour with no cold joints.
Sand and smooth the plaster surface as needed.
Apply grout to the new tile.
Reinstall all plastic covers/fittings in the pool and spa.
Replace pool and spa lights (white or color-changing, per customer selection).
Replace the pump, filter, heater, and associated plumbing in the equipment area, if included in the scope.
Fill the pool completely with water.
Test the equipment system to confirm proper operation.
Advise the customer to perform the plaster start-up chemical balancing within 2 weeks of filling.
notes: Plaster must be completed in one continuous session — no joints — or the finish will show visible seams and is subject to premature failure.
Customer must complete the chemical start-up process no later than 2 weeks after refilling to protect the new plaster finish.

jobTypes.name: Rain Gutters
jobTypes.summary: Removal of failing gutters and installation of new sloped rain gutters and downspouts to direct roof water away from the foundation.
steps: Remove existing rain gutters, if any; clean up and haul away debris.
Install new aluminum, vinyl, or metal rain gutters at a 3% slope, in the customer's selected color.
Install color-matched downspouts at intervals of approximately every 30 feet.
Direct downspout discharge away from the foundation.
Clean up and haul away debris.
exclusions: Downspout extensions/drainage tie-in to a French drain (see French Drain if needed)
notes: Downspouts should discharge onto a splash block, extension, or tied drain line — never directly against the foundation.

jobTypes.name: Whole-House Repipe
jobTypes.summary: Replacement of old galvanized or failing supply piping throughout the house with new copper (or PEX) piping, up to current code.
steps: Remove existing galvanized or deteriorated supply piping.
Run and install new copper (or PEX) supply piping up to code to every fixture with a water source: kitchen, bathrooms, laundry, and water heater.
Install pipe straps and seismic retrofit clips per code.
Install foam insulation on all exposed hot water piping.
Connect all fixtures throughout the house to the new piping.
Replace the main line from the city meter to the house if it remains galvanized.
Patch and paint walls opened for the repipe (local match only).
Test the system for leaks and proper pressure.
Final inspection.
Haul away all old piping and debris.
exclusions: Full repaint beyond the patched wall sections
notes: On a raised foundation, repipe lines typically run underneath the house; on a slab foundation, they typically run through the attic — this affects both access and drywall/ceiling patching scope.
Patch-and-paint after repipe is a local match only — an exact match to existing wall paint is not guaranteed.

jobTypes.name: Re-Drain (Waste/Drain Line Replacement)
jobTypes.summary: Replacement of cracked, bellied, or corroded waste/drain lines under the house and to the main sewer connection with new ABS piping.
steps: Excavate under the house to access existing drain lines.
Disconnect existing drain connections throughout the house — toilets, sinks, and laundry — section by section.
Install new ABS drain piping to replace the old lines.
Connect all fixtures to the new ABS piping.
Replace the wax ring at every toilet.
Excavate the main drain line to the city connection, approximately 5 feet deep, as required.
Install new ABS piping for the main line and connect it to the city sewer connection.
Install a code-compliant cleanout access point.
Perform a water test on the completed system to confirm no leaks.
Backfill all excavated areas.
Haul away all old piping and debris.
Clean the entire work area.
Final inspection.
notes: A camera inspection before quoting confirms the extent of cracked, root-infiltrated, or bellied sections and whether trench or trenchless repair applies.

jobTypes.name: House Rewire
jobTypes.summary: Replacement of old, undersized, or unsafe house wiring — including knob-and-tube or cloth/aluminum wiring — with new code-compliant Romex wiring, outlets, and circuits.
steps: Open all existing outlets, switches, and fixture boxes.
Replace all junction boxes throughout the house for switches, outlets, and light fixtures.
Fish new Romex wiring through walls and ceilings, replacing old wiring.
Remove and haul away all old wiring.
Run dedicated circuits to code for major appliances and high-draw rooms.
Install GFCI/AFCI protection and grounded circuits/outlets where required by code.
Add outlets as needed to bring the house up to current code spacing requirements.
Install hardwired, interconnected smoke and carbon monoxide detectors.
Install new switches and outlets throughout.
Create dedicated breakers in the panel up to code.
Connect all new circuits to the main panel.
Patch all walls opened for wiring access.
Patch and paint — local match only, not a full-room repaint.
Clean the entire work area.
Final inspection.
exclusions: Electrical panel upgrade if the existing panel lacks capacity (see Electrical Panel Upgrade)
Full repaint beyond the patched wall sections
notes: Patch-and-paint after rewiring is a local match only — an exact match to existing wall paint and texture is not guaranteed.
Homes with knob-and-tube or cloth-insulated wiring often carry an insurability flag — a completed rewire with permit sign-off resolves this for the homeowner's carrier.

jobTypes.name: Retaining Wall
jobTypes.summary: Construction of a new engineered retaining wall to hold back a slope or grade change, using reinforced concrete and block.
steps: Demolish any existing wall or fence and prepare the surface for the new retaining wall.
Excavate an 18-inch by 18-inch footing trench.
Install 1/4-inch rebar inside the trench in an X pattern every 3 feet vertically.
Install 1/4-inch horizontal rebar tied to the vertical rebar.
Pour 2,500 PSI concrete into the trench to the top of the footing.
Set the first course of concrete block into the wet concrete before it cures, sinking each block halfway into the footing.
Install vertical 1/4-inch rebar inside the block cores to the full height of the wall, and continue coursing block with mortar between each unit.
Pour 2,500 PSI concrete inside the block cores to fully solidify the wall.
Install finished cap blocks with mortar along the top of the wall.
Apply a stucco finish to the wall face in the customer's selected color.
Install drainage behind the wall (weep holes and/or gravel backfill) per engineering.
Clean the entire job site and haul away debris.
Final inspection.
exclusions: Engineering/soils report if required for wall height (quoted separately)
notes: Retaining walls over 4 feet (measured from the bottom of the footing) require engineering and a building permit in nearly all SoCal jurisdictions — confirm exact height triggers with the local building department.
Proper drainage behind the wall is required to prevent hydrostatic pressure failure — do not omit weep holes/gravel backfill.

jobTypes.name: Roofing — Composition Shingle
jobTypes.summary: Full tear-off of an existing composition/asphalt shingle roof and installation of a new shingle roofing system.
steps: Demolish the complete existing roof covering and remove all debris.
Clean the job site of removed material.
Repair or replace damaged roof sheathing (see Re-Decking if extensive).
Replace all metal roof vent covers.
Replace or install new attic vents per current code.
Install new flashing at all roof-to-wall and valley transitions.
Install synthetic waterproof underlayment across the roof deck.
Install new 30-year composition shingles in the customer's selected brand and color.
Seal around all roof penetrations (vents, pipes) with lifetime-rated silicone sealant.
Clean the entire job site.
Final inspection.
exclusions: Sheathing/re-decking beyond minor spot repair (see Re-Decking)
notes: Any rotted or delaminated sheathing discovered during tear-off is a change order — quote re-decking separately and get customer sign-off before proceeding.

jobTypes.name: Roofing — Tile
jobTypes.summary: Removal of an existing tile roof, replacement of underlayment, and reinstallation of tile (with broken tile replacement as needed).
steps: Remove all tile from the roof and stage for reuse where sound.
Demolish and remove the old underlayment.
Repair or replace damaged roof sheathing as needed.
Install new waterproof underlayment across the entire roof surface.
Replace all metal roof vent covers.
Replace or install new attic vents per current code.
Install new flashing at all roof-to-wall and valley transitions.
Reinstall sound existing tile and replace broken tile as needed (extra cost for tile replacement).
Seal around all roof penetrations with lifetime-rated silicone sealant.
Clean the entire job site.
Final inspection.
selections: Replacement tile match (if extensive breakage) — style/color
exclusions: Replacement tile beyond minor breakage (extra cost)
Sheathing/re-decking beyond minor spot repair (see Re-Decking)
notes: Tile roofs are commonly underlayment failures with sound tile on top — reusing existing tile keeps costs down, but broken/cracked tile replacement is billed as an extra since exact matches for older tile profiles can be hard to source.

jobTypes.name: Roofing — Flat/Torch-Down
jobTypes.summary: Tear-off or overlay of a failed low-slope roof membrane and installation of a new torch-down or hot-mop roofing system.
steps: Demolish the complete existing roof layers and remove all debris (tear-off), or prepare the existing surface for an overlay if condition allows.
Clean the job site.
Repair or replace damaged roof sheathing as needed.
Install new metal roof vent covers.
Replace or install new attic vents per current code.
Install new flashing at all roof-to-wall transitions and penetrations.
Install a base layer of fiberglass-based underlayment (tar paper).
Apply a first hot-mop layer and roll it to create a flat, sealed surface.
Apply a second hot-mop/torch-down layer at 1/2-inch thickness.
Install fiberglass cap sheets as the finished surface.
Install new metal flashing on all pipes and vents.
Clean the entire job site.
Final inspection.
selections: Torch-down vs. hot-mop system (per roof condition and budget)
exclusions: Sheathing/re-decking beyond minor spot repair (see Re-Decking)
notes: Overlay is only appropriate when the existing surface is sound and dry — a moisture-compromised low-slope roof should be fully torn off, not overlaid.

jobTypes.name: Roof Re-Decking
jobTypes.summary: Replacement of rotted, delaminated, or undersized roof sheathing discovered during a roofing tear-off, before new roofing can be installed.
steps: Remove the existing deteriorated roof layer down to the framing, clean up, and haul away debris.
Install new CDX plywood sheathing sized to code (typically 1/2-inch minimum).
Install ventilation pipe boots/curbs as needed.
Install 30-lb tar paper underlayment over the new sheathing.
Install new edge metal along the roof perimeter.
Install new roofing material to match the surrounding roof (30/50-year fiberglass shingles or per the parent roofing scope).
Install new metal flashing on all pipes and vents.
Clean up and haul away debris.
exclusions: The parent roofing system beyond the re-decked area (quoted as part of the main roofing scope)
notes: Re-decking is almost never sold as a standalone job — it's typically discovered mid-tear-off on a roofing project. Present it to the customer as a change order with photos of the exposed damage before proceeding, since it affects both cost and schedule.
jobTypes.durationHint: 1-3 days (as a change order within a roofing job)

jobTypes.name: Stucco: New
jobTypes.summary: Application of a complete new three-coat stucco system to exterior walls, typically over new framing or after removal of a failed existing system.
steps: Excavate a 6-inch deep trench around the perimeter of the house.
Remove all old stucco down to the framing, if applicable.
Install waterproof paper and wire lath (chicken wire) over the wall sheathing, nailed to the framing.
Apply the scratch (base) coat of cement and allow it to cure for approximately 6 days.
Apply the brown (second) coat of cement and allow it to cure for approximately 4 days.
Apply the color coat in the customer's selected texture and color.
Prepare trim for paint: sand, caulk, and patch as needed.
Prime and paint trim.
Backfill the trench around the house.
Clean up and haul away debris.
Final inspection.
inclusions: Wire lath, paper, and cement coats
notes: Each stucco coat needs its full cure time before the next coat is applied — do not compress this schedule, as it compromises crack resistance.
jobTypes.durationHint: 2-3 weeks including cure time between coats

jobTypes.name: Stucco Repair & Re-Stucco
jobTypes.summary: Patch repair of isolated cracked/spalled stucco, or a full re-stucco of the entire exterior where damage is widespread.
steps: Cover all windows, trees, bushes, planters, and patio furniture.
Excavate a 6-inch deep trench around the perimeter of the house.
For full re-stucco: sand-blast or hydro-blast the existing stucco (per local jurisdiction requirements) to key the surface.
For patch repair: scrape and remove the damaged stucco section only.
Clean up and haul away debris.
Prepare walls for the brown coat: fix cracks and patch as needed.
Apply the new stucco coat(s) in the customer's selected color and texture.
Prepare trim for painting: sand, caulk, and patch as needed.
Prime and paint all trim.
Backfill the trench around the house.
Clean the entire job site.
inclusions: Cement coats and lath (where the wall is opened to bare sheathing)
notes: An exact color/texture match to existing weathered stucco cannot be guaranteed on patch repairs — this is disclosed to the customer as a local match only.
Isolated cracking is typically a patch scope; widespread spalling/delamination across most of the exterior calls for a full re-stucco, which is priced and scheduled differently.

jobTypes.name: Solar PV Installation
jobTypes.summary: Design, permitting, and installation of a rooftop solar photovoltaic system sized to the customer's electrical load.
steps: Perform a sun/shade analysis of the roof to determine optimal panel placement and expected production.
Prepare a drawing set specifying panel locations and system components.
Submit plans to the city building and safety and electrical departments for permit approval.
Submit plans to the fire department for setback/access compliance.
Submit plans to the utility company for interconnection review.
Install mounting rails, brackets, and roof-penetration flashing/waterproofing.
Install the solar panels per the approved system size.
Install the combiner box, DC disconnect, and AC disconnect.
Install conduit for all system wiring.
Install wiring and connections per the system design.
Coordinate the utility interconnection and permission-to-operate approval.
Final inspection.
exclusions: Electrical panel upgrade if capacity is insufficient (see Electrical Panel Upgrade)
Roof replacement if the existing roof is near end-of-life (address roofing first)
notes: Install the panels according to city code and manufacturer specifications; roof penetrations must be properly flashed and waterproofed to avoid future leaks.
If the roof is more than a few years from replacement, recommend re-roofing before solar installation to avoid removing and reinstalling panels later.

jobTypes.name: Termite & Dry-Rot Treatment
jobTypes.summary: Whole-house tent fumigation or localized organic treatment to eliminate an active termite infestation, often paired with repair of termite/dry-rot damaged framing.
steps: Coordinate a Wood-Destroying Organism (WDO) inspection to confirm infestation extent and identify damaged framing.
Remove and cover all food and food-preparation equipment in the house.
Coordinate with the gas company to shut off the gas valve before tenting.
Cover the entire house with a fumigation tent.
Fumigate the house with the certified treatment gas; the house must remain unoccupied for 3-4 days.
Remove the fumigation tent.
Remove all protective plastic sheeting from the interior.
Return all covered items to their original locations.
Coordinate with the gas company to restore gas service.
Repair termite/dry-rot damaged framing, siding, or trim identified in the WDO inspection.
selections: Fumigation vs. organic/localized treatment (based on infestation scope)
inclusions: Fumigation gas and tenting (or organic treatment chemical)
exclusions: Structural repair of damaged framing (quoted as a separate scope based on WDO findings)
Permit and city fees where applicable
notes: Organic/localized treatment (orange oil or similar) is injected directly into infested framing at multiple points and does not require tenting or vacating the house, but it does not carry the same whole-structure guarantee as fumigation — set expectations with the customer up front.
A completion certificate/inspection report is provided after either treatment method for real estate or insurance purposes.
jobTypes.durationHint: 3-5 days (fumigation) / 1 day (organic treatment), plus repair time

jobTypes.name: New Interior & Exterior Walls
jobTypes.summary: Framing and finishing of new interior or exterior walls, including any structural post/beam work required to support the change.
steps: Reinforce ceiling-to-roof framing per engineering before removing any existing wall that will be relocated or removed.
Demolish existing walls being removed or altered, if any.
Install the sole (bottom) plate horizontally per the approved layout.
Install vertical 2x4 studs at 16 inches on center.
Install posts (4x4, 6x4, 8x4, or steel as specified) at any point carrying concentrated load, per engineering.
Install the upper (top) plate horizontally.
Rough in electrical wiring and/or plumbing through the new framing, drilled per code limits.
Install framing connector brackets at engineered connection points.
Install plywood sheathing on the wall where required for shear strength or fire separation.
Install exterior sheathing on the outward face of new exterior walls, if applicable.
Install drywall: green board/waterproof-rated in kitchens and bathrooms, fire-rated board where required, screwed every 8 inches on center.
Tape and finish all drywall joints.
Install baseboards and crown molding per customer selection.
Apply interior paint.
Apply exterior stucco to complete new exterior walls.
Clean up and haul away debris.
Final inspection.
exclusions: Engineering/structural calculations (quoted separately if not already provided)
Flooring transition/patch beyond the new wall footprint
notes: All new or altered walls must follow engineered blueprints, particularly where they replace a load-bearing wall — never remove a load-bearing wall without an engineered beam/post plan in place first.

jobTypes.name: Balcony / Deck Waterproofing
jobTypes.summary: Removal of a deteriorated waterproof deck surface down to the framing and installation of a new multi-layer waterproof coating system, addressing California's exterior elevated element (EEE) inspection requirements.
steps: Remove the deteriorated waterproof surface and plywood down to the floor joists.
Inspect exposed framing for rot or damage and replace/sister any compromised joists.
Install new 3/4-inch CDX plywood decking.
Remove existing scuppers/drains.
Install new 2x3 scuppers sloped to drain water off the deck.
Install the first layer of the waterproofing system: fiberglass-reinforced diamond lath.
Apply two coats of resin-based leveling compound over the lath.
Embed 3/4 oz. fiberglass reinforcing material into the leveling compound.
Apply one coat of acrylic skim coat.
Apply one coat of acrylic texture coat.
Apply two coats of acrylic finish resin in the color selected by the owner.
Clean up and haul away debris.
Final inspection.
inclusions: Plywood, scuppers, and waterproofing system material
notes: Buildings with three or more multifamily units are subject to California SB326 (condos/HOAs) or SB721 (apartments) mandatory exterior elevated element inspection cycles — confirm whether this project falls under either statute and its inspection deadline.
Framing found to be rotted or water-damaged during demo is a common change order — inspect and photograph before covering with new plywood.

jobTypes.name: Windows: Retrofit (Vinyl & Aluminum)
jobTypes.summary: Replacement of existing windows with new insulated vinyl or aluminum windows installed over the existing frame (retrofit method, no stucco disturbance).
steps: Remove the existing window sash and hardware, leaving the existing frame in place; clean up and haul away debris.
Prepare the existing window frame to receive the new retrofit unit.

Install the new double-pane, insulated retrofit window (Argon gas, Low-E glass) over the existing frame in the customer's selected material (vinyl or aluminum) and color (white, almond, black, or bronze).
Apply 50-year rated caulking completely around the frame per code.
Clean up and haul away debris.
selections: Frame color: white, almond, black, or bronze
exclusions: Stucco patch/repair (not required for standard retrofit; quoted separately if the opening must be resized)
notes: Retrofit windows reuse the existing frame and do not disturb surrounding stucco, making this the faster and less invasive option compared to new-construction replacement.
jobTypes.durationHint: 1-3 days for a typical whole-house window replacement

jobTypes.name: Windows: New Construction
jobTypes.summary: Full removal of existing windows and frames down to the rough opening, with new windows installed and surrounding stucco rebuilt — used when resizing openings or matching new construction.
steps: Remove the existing window and frame completely, exposing the rough opening framing; clean up and haul away debris.
Install the new wood, aluminum, or vinyl window (new-construction style) in the customer's selected material and color.
Install waterproof paper and wire lath around the new window opening.
Apply a cement scratch coat and brown coat around the window.
Apply the color-coat stucco finish to match the surrounding exterior.
Clean up and haul away debris.
Final inspection.
selections: Frame material: wood, aluminum, or vinyl
Frame color: white, almond, black, or bronze
inclusions: Window units, framing material, and stucco patch around the opening
exclusions: Full house re-stucco beyond the immediate window openings (see Re-Stucco if widespread)
notes: New-construction windows are the right choice when resizing an opening, correcting rot at the frame, or matching a new-construction look — expect a wider stucco disturbance and patch radius than a retrofit install.
For masonry/block openings, wood or foil trim is installed around the window in place of a full stucco return — confirm the wall type before quoting.
jobTypes.durationHint: 2-5 days for a typical whole-house window replacement

jobTypes.name: Garage-to-ADU Conversion
jobTypes.summary: Conversion of an existing attached or detached garage into a livable accessory dwelling unit, including a bedroom and full bathroom, structural upgrades, and all new utilities.
steps: Mask, cover, and protect the surrounding areas of the work zone.

Demolish designated interior and exterior wall sections, the existing water heater, and any fixtures in the conversion footprint; demolish, frame-close, or resize existing window openings per approved plans; haul away all debris.

Frame any new addition square footage tied into the existing garage footprint, including new load-bearing posts and a flush beam where an existing wall/beam is removed, per approved architectural and engineering plans.
Install shear walls per engineering plans.
Frame in new window openings and install new dual-pane windows; frame in and install new interior doors and a new exterior door per the plans.
Install wall insulation (R-13 minimum) in all new addition walls and any wall shared with the existing house.
Relocate the electrical panel and/or gas line/meter to their new approved locations if required by the utility.
Install a new tankless water heater in the approved location.
Rough in new drain lines for the shower, toilet, and vanity, connected to the existing sewer line per code, and run new copper water supply lines to all new bathroom and kitchen fixtures.
Rough in electrical: exhaust fan, GFCI outlets, switches, and recessed/fixture lighting per the approved lighting layout, plus new smoke and carbon monoxide detectors.
Hot-mop the new shower pan and perform a standing water test.
Install cement board or floating cement on shower walls and bathroom floor, then install customer-selected tile (customer-supplied).
Install new drywall on all framed walls and ceilings; tape, texture, and prime.
Paint all new interior surfaces.
Install and connect the vanity, faucets, toilet, showerhead, and bathroom accessories (customer-supplied).

Install new unfinished wood flooring in the addition area and patch into the existing floor at the tie-in line; install baseboards and door trim to match the existing house where standard sizes are available.
Apply new stucco to match the existing house exterior as closely as possible.
Install a new torch-down roof over the addition area.
Pour a new concrete landing and steps at the new exterior door.
Run new duct work from the addition to tie into the existing central HVAC system.
Complete a final walkthrough with the customer and address punch-list touch-ups.
Provide the required unconditional waiver and release upon final payment.
Final inspection and city sign-off.
selections: Flooring finish (customer arranges sanding/staining of new unfinished wood)
inclusions: Structural observation by an engineer for wall removal
exclusions: Plumbing fixtures, tile material, shower glass, and vanity (customer-supplied)
Wood flooring finishing (sanding, stain, clear coat)
Non-recessed light fixtures and window treatments
notes: A structural engineer must sign off on any wall removal before demolition proceeds — this is included in the scope.
Pre-1978 garages require a lead survey, and any suspect material should be tested for asbestos before demolition.

The customer is responsible for delays caused by missing or incorrectly ordered finishing items noted under exclusions — build a clear late-fee/schedule-adjustment clause into the contract, as is standard for ADU projects of this size.

jobTypes.name: Room Addition (Ground Floor)
jobTypes.summary: Expansion of the existing house footprint with a new ground-floor room — bedroom, family room, or expanded kitchen — structurally tied into the existing house.
steps: Finalize architectural plans and structural engineering for the addition (see Blueprints & Plans).
Pull the city building permit.
Demolish the exterior wall section where the addition ties into the existing house.
Excavate and pour new footings and foundation for the addition per engineering.
Frame the new floor system, walls, and roof structure, tying into the existing house framing per engineered connection details.
Install any new load-bearing posts, beams, and shear walls specified by engineering.
Install roof sheathing and a weatherproof underlayment/roofing tie-in to the existing roof.
Install new windows and exterior doors per the approved plans.
Rough in electrical, and plumbing if the addition includes a kitchen/bathroom.
Install wall and ceiling insulation.
Install drywall throughout the addition; tape, texture, and prime.
Apply new stucco to match the existing house exterior as closely as possible.
Install flooring selected by the customer, tying into the existing adjoining floor where applicable.
Install baseboards and trim to match the existing house.
Paint the interior of the addition.
Install light fixtures, switches, and outlets.
Complete a final walkthrough with the customer and address punch-list items.
Clean the job site and haul away all debris.
Final inspection and city sign-off.
exclusions: Architectural plans and engineering (see Blueprints & Plans, unless bundled)
Kitchen/bath fixtures and finish material (customer selection)
notes: Tying new framing and roofing into the existing structure is the highest-risk phase for hidden conditions — old framing, deteriorated sheathing, or undersized existing members are common change orders once the wall is opened.
Verify setback and lot coverage limits with the zoning gate reference before finalizing the footprint.

jobTypes.name: Water Heater Replacement (Tank & Tankless)
jobTypes.summary: Replacement of a failed or aging tank water heater with a new like-for-like unit, or conversion to a tankless system.
steps: Shut off water, gas/electric, and drain the existing water heater.
Disconnect and remove the existing unit; haul away.
Verify and upgrade the venting, gas line sizing, or electrical circuit as needed for the new unit.
Set the new tank unit on a code-compliant stand/pan, or mount the new tankless unit on the wall.
Connect water supply lines with new shut-off valves.
Connect the gas line with a new flexible connector and sediment trap, or connect the dedicated electrical circuit for electric units.
Install/verify the temperature and pressure relief (T&P) valve and discharge line.
Install seismic strapping per California code.
For tankless conversions: install a condensate drain line if the unit is condensing type.
Fill the system, test for leaks, and verify proper operation and temperature.
Clean the work area and haul away the old unit.
Final inspection.
exclusions: Gas line upsizing beyond the water heater run, if the whole-house line requires upgrade
notes: California code requires seismic strapping (two straps minimum) on all water heaters — verify this on every replacement, even like-for-like swaps.
Tankless conversions often require a larger gas line and dedicated venting — confirm both before quoting a flat conversion price.

jobTypes.name: Electrical Panel Upgrade (200A)
jobTypes.summary: Replacement of an undersized, obsolete, or recalled electrical panel with a new 200-amp panel sized for current and future loads.
steps: Coordinate a temporary power shutdown with the utility company.
Remove the existing main panel and any recalled/obsolete equipment (e.g., Zinsco, Federal Pacific breakers).
Install a new 200-amp rated panel and meter enclosure.
Reconnect and re-label all existing circuits to the new panel.
Install new grounding and bonding per current code.
Add new breaker spaces/sub-feeds for anticipated loads (EV charger, heat pump, solar, ADU) as scoped with the customer.
Install a new service disconnect if required by the utility.
Coordinate utility meter reconnection.
Test all circuits for proper operation.
Clean the work area and haul away the old panel.
Final inspection.
selections: Additional circuit reservations for future EV/solar/heat pump loads
inclusions: New panel, breakers, and grounding material
exclusions: Downstream rewiring of existing circuits (see House Rewire if needed)
notes: Zinsco and Federal Pacific panels are known fire-risk brands still common in older SoCal homes — flag these for priority replacement even absent a specific complaint.
Plan spare breaker capacity for EV chargers, heat pumps, and solar interconnection even if the customer isn't installing them yet — it's far cheaper to reserve space now than to upsize again later.

jobTypes.name: EV Charger Circuit
jobTypes.summary: Installation of a dedicated 240V circuit from the electrical panel to the garage or driveway for a Level 2 EV charger.
steps: Verify available panel capacity for a new dedicated 240V circuit (see Electrical Panel Upgrade if capacity is insufficient).
Run new wiring from the panel to the charger location, routed through conduit where exposed.
Install a new dedicated breaker sized to the charger's amperage requirement.
Install a NEMA outlet or hardwire connection point per the customer's charger model.
Mount the charger unit (if customer-supplied) or a junction box for future charger installation.
Test the circuit for proper voltage and operation.
Patch any wall/stucco penetrations from the wiring run.
Clean the work area and haul away debris.
Final inspection.
exclusions: The EV charger unit itself (customer-supplied unless separately quoted)
Panel upgrade if capacity is insufficient (quoted separately)
notes: Confirm the charger's amperage draw against remaining panel capacity before quoting — many older 100A panels cannot support a Level 2 charger alongside existing loads without an upgrade.

jobTypes.name: Sewer Lateral Replacement (Trench vs. Trenchless)
jobTypes.summary: Replacement of a collapsed, root-infiltrated, or badly offset sewer lateral between the house and the city main, via open trench or trenchless pipe-bursting/lining method.
steps: Perform a camera inspection to confirm the location and extent of the failed lateral.
